skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, revising, and testing AI skills with a TDD-style workflow before deployment.
skill.summary: Guides AI skill authors through test-driven skill design, verification, and deployment checks.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, official reference text, URLs, and illustrative paths. The confirmed risk is a bundled Node utility that executes Graphviz and writes output files in a user-selected skill directory, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: Bundled Script Executes Graphviz
medium_findings.description: The package includes render-graphs.js, which imports child_process and runs the dot command with diagram content supplied through standard input. The command string is fixed and does not interpolate user input, so this is not command injection, but it still executes a local external binary when the script is run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable behavior is directly visible in the script. Confidence is not higher because the command is fixed and appears to support the documented diagram-rendering feature.

medium_findings.title: Script Writes Generated Files Under User Selected Directory
medium_findings.description: The render helper resolves the user-provided skill directory, reads its SKILL.md file, creates a diagrams directory, and writes SVG or dot output files. This is expected behavior for a rendering utility, but users should know the script has filesystem side effects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem reads and writes are explicit and tied to user input. The behavior is legitimate for the tool, but it can modify files outside the current skill if pointed there.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command and Code Examples Trigger Static Command Rules
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are Markdown examples, code fences, or authoring guidance rather than executable package behavior. Examples include SKILL.md structure snippets, official best-practice snippets, and testing scenario text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced examples or prose, not active code paths. This explains a large share of the duplicated static analyzer matches.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The network findings are documentation links and image references in a copied best-practices guide. I did not find code that sends data to these URLs or performs runtime network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL evidence appears inside Markdown reference content. There is no accompanying fetch, curl, request, or network library usage in the reviewed executable file.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Paths Are Illustrative Skill Locations
low_findings.description: The hidden-file findings point to examples such as ~/.claude/skills and ~/.codex/skills. These paths describe where personal skills live or how test scenarios might search skills; they are not automatic reads of hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are embedded in documentation and test examples. The only executable file reviewed does not read those home-directory paths automatically.

low_findings.title: Pressure Scenario Prompts Are Test Fixtures, Not Audit Override Instructions
low_findings.description: The skill includes prompts that tell a test agent to choose and act under pressure. They are used as test fixtures for skill evaluation, and I found no text instructing the marketplace auditor to ignore rules, skip review, or change the risk level.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is intentionally directive, so it deserves review. Context shows it is framed as a scenario inside Markdown examples, not as an attempt to control this audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Process Execution via execSync
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js uses execSync to call Graphviz dot and to check for the dot binary. The command is fixed, but local process execution is still a sensitive capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed by direct code. The risk is limited because no user-controlled shell string is concatenated into the command.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes from Render Helper
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js creates a diagrams directory and writes generated SVG and dot files. This is a clear filesystem side effect that should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are explicit and easy to verify. They are expected for a diagram renderer but remain relevant to marketplace risk labeling.

content.value_statement: AI skills often fail because authors do not test whether agents use them under pressure. This skill provides a TDD-style process for writing, testing, and refining skills before deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to structure SKILL.md files with concise metadata and searchable triggers.
Maps RED-GREEN-REFACTOR practices to skill creation and revision workflows.
Provides pressure-scenario testing methods for discipline-enforcing skills.
Defines when to use inline guidance, supporting reference files, and utility scripts.
Includes Graphviz conventions and a helper script for rendering dot flowcharts.
Shows how to close loopholes by capturing agent rationalizations and retesting.
limitations: It assumes the author already understands test-driven development concepts.
It is focused on AI skill documentation, not general software documentation.
The bundled rendering script requires Graphviz to be installed separately.
It does not automatically validate skill quality without manual scenario testing.

use_cases.title: Create a New Discipline Skill
use_cases.description: Design a skill that makes agents follow a required process even when speed or sunk cost creates pressure.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Skill
use_cases.description: Revise unclear skill guidance after testing shows agents skip steps, find loopholes, or misunderstand triggers.

use_cases.title: Prepare Skills for Deployment
use_cases.description: Check structure, examples, flowcharts, and testing evidence before publishing a skill for repeated agent use.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Skill Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new skill outline.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-skills guidance to draft a new skill for this workflow: [describe workflow]. Include the trigger description, overview, core pattern, and common mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Pressure Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request to test whether a skill works under pressure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three pressure scenarios for this skill: [paste skill goal]. Combine time pressure, authority pressure, and sunk cost. Include clear choices for each scenario.

prompt_templates.title: Refine After Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request after an agent bypasses a skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this failed skill test: [paste scenario and agent response]. Identify the rationalization, update the rule language, and propose a re-test.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill before deployment: [paste skill]. Check trigger quality, structure, examples, testing coverage, loopholes, and whether supporting files are justified.

output_examples.input: A user wants to create a skill for mandatory code review after implementation.
output: A trigger-focused description that avoids summarizing the whole workflow.
A pressure scenario where speed and confidence tempt the agent to skip review.
A rationalization table covering common excuses for skipping review.

output_examples.input: A skill test shows the agent chose a shortcut despite reading the skill.
output: The shortcut is recorded as a failing test case.
The skill gains an explicit counter-rule for that rationalization.
The same scenario is re-run to verify the behavior changed.

output_examples.input: A skill includes a complex decision process that is hard to scan.
output: A recommendation to add one small flowchart for the decision point.
Graphviz style guidance for node shapes and labels.
A reminder to keep reference material in tables or lists instead.

best_practices: Run baseline scenarios before writing or editing the skill so failures are based on observed behavior.
Keep the skill description focused on when to use the skill, not the full process.
Retest after each change until agents follow the rule under realistic pressure.
anti_patterns: Do not create skills for one-off project stories that will not be reused.
Do not use long descriptions that encourage agents to skip the full skill body.
Do not deploy a discipline skill without pressure testing and documented rationalizations.

faq.question: What does this skill help me write?
faq.answer: It helps write AI skills that document reusable techniques, workflows, references, and discipline rules.

faq.question: Why does it use a TDD workflow?
faq.answer: The workflow proves the skill addresses real agent failures instead of assumptions about what agents might miss.

faq.question: Can I use it for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is written for agent skill authoring and mentions personal skill locations for Claude Code and Codex.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Node script that renders Graphviz dot blocks to SVG files when run manually.

faq.question: Is it only for new skills?
faq.answer: No. It also covers editing existing skills, closing loopholes, and retesting after revisions.

faq.question: When should I avoid using it?
faq.answer: Avoid it for simple project notes, standard references already documented elsewhere, or rules that can be enforced automatically.
